originalTitle: Sudan army denies deadly Eid strike on key hospital in Darfur
title: A Big Wish for Peace in Sudan!
content:
Hello, Friends!

Hello, amazing friends! Today, your kid-friendly news reporter from Africa is here to share some important news. It’s a little bit sad, but it also reminds us how important it is to be kind and wish for peace in the world. This story comes from a big country in Africa called Sudan. Sudan is a beautiful place, full of warm sunshine, long rivers, and many wonderful people, including lots of children just like you!

Where is Sudan?

Sudan is a very, very big country. It's in the part of Africa that has lots of desert, but also green places where the famous Nile River flows. Imagine a river so long it goes through many countries! That's the Nile. Many children in Sudan love to play games, go to school, and spend time with their families, just like you do. They have dreams and hopes for the future. Did you know? The Nile River is one of the longest rivers in the whole world! It’s like a super long blue ribbon flowing across the land.

A Very Special Place: The Hospital

In every town and city, there are special buildings called hospitals. Do you know what a hospital is for? It’s a place where very kind doctors and super-helpful nurses work together to make sick people better. If you fall down and hurt your knee, or if you have a bad cough, a hospital is where you can go to get help. Hospitals are like big, safe homes for people who need healing. They are full of hope and care. Did you know? Doctors and nurses are like everyday superheroes! They work very hard to keep everyone healthy and safe, even in difficult times.

A Sad Day Near the Hospital

Sometimes, even in good places, people have big fights. When people fight with loud noises and strong weapons, it's called war. And war can make many people very sad and hurt. A very sad thing happened recently in a part of Sudan called Darfur. There was a big, loud noise very close to a hospital. This made many people very scared. Sadly, because of this loud noise near the hospital, many people were hurt, and some even lost their lives. It makes us very, very sad to hear this. The news tells us that sixty-four people were hurt or lost their lives. Can you imagine? Among them were thirteen children, just like some of you sitting at home. There were also two kind nurses and one brave doctor, people who work every day to help others.

What is Eid? A Time for Happiness

What makes this story even sadder is that it happened during a very special time called Eid. Eid is a huge celebration for many families in Sudan and all around the world! It's like a giant, happy party where everyone gets together. Families share delicious food, wear new clothes, give gifts, and play games. It's a time for joy, peace, and being grateful. Imagine having a big family celebration, and then something sad happens. That's why it's extra sad that this loud noise happened during Eid, a time that should be full of smiles and laughter. It reminds us how much we need peace. Did you know? There are two main Eid celebrations each year, and they are both about coming together, sharing, and spreading happiness!

Why Hospitals Must Be Safe

Hospitals are very, very important places. They are places of healing. When someone is sick, they need to feel safe to get better. Hospitals should always be protected. Everyone, everywhere, agrees that hospitals are special places that should never, ever be part of any fighting. They are neutral zones, which means they don't take sides in fights; they just help anyone who needs it, no matter who they are. Imagine if your school or your playground wasn't safe. That's why we always want hospitals to be places of peace and healing for everyone. They give hope to people who are unwell and need a lot of care. Did you know? The idea that hospitals must always be safe, even during wars, is a rule that many countries around the world try to follow! It's called humanitarian law.

Working for Peace

When sad things like this happen, it reminds us how much we need peace in the world. Peace means that people are kind to each other, they talk instead of fight, and everyone feels safe. We all wish for the fighting in Sudan to stop. We wish for all the children in Sudan to be safe, to play, to laugh, and to go to school without any worries. Every child deserves a peaceful life, where they can grow up happy and strong. Even you can help spread peace! How? By being kind to your friends, sharing your toys, and helping your family. Small acts of kindness can grow into big waves of peace! Did you know? When children play together, no matter where they are from, they are already helping to build a more peaceful world!

People Who Help

Even when sad things happen, there are always good people trying to help. Many kind people and groups from all over the world are working to send food, medicine, and blankets to places like Sudan. They want to make sure that everyone, especially children, has what they need to be safe and healthy. They are like helpers who bring light into dark times. These helpers remind us that even when things are tough, there's always hope and people who care deeply about others. They are trying their best to make sure the hospitals can keep helping people and that children can live happily. Did you know? Many people who help others in faraway lands are called 'aid workers,' and they are very brave and kind!

Our Hope for the Future

So, my friends, today we heard about a sad event in Sudan, near a hospital. It reminds us that fighting only brings sadness. But it also reminds us about the importance of hope, kindness, and peace. Let’s all remember to be kind to each other, to share, and to wish for peace for everyone, everywhere. Especially for the children in Sudan. Every child deserves to be safe, to play, to learn, and to have a happy life. Let’s send our good thoughts and wishes to Sudan. May peace come very soon, like a warm, gentle sunrise after a long night.
summary: A sad event happened near a hospital in Sudan during a happy holiday called Eid, where many people, including 13 children, two nurses, and a doctor, were hurt or lost their lives, reminding us all to wish for peace and kindness.
whyMatters: This news matters because hospitals should always be safe places for sick people, especially doctors and nurses who help everyone get better, and children everywhere deserve to be safe and happy, not scared by fighting, reminding us how important peace is.

originalTitle: 'Peace is a gradual thing': How land, cattle and identity fuel a deadly Nigerian conflict
title: 'Peace is a gradual thing': How land, cattle and identity fuel a deadly Nigerian conflict
content: How a lack of justice and trust in the security forces perpetuates deadly violence in Plateau state.

originalTitle: Rwanda batter becomes youngest women's T20 centurion
title: Rwanda's Young Cricket Star Makes History!
content:
Hello, Super Friends! Meet Fanny, Our African Cricket Hero!

Hello, amazing friends! Do you know about super stars? Not stars in the sky, but super people who do amazing things! We have a super star right here in Africa. She is from a beautiful country called Rwanda. Her name is Fanny. Fanny Utagushimaninde is her full name. She is a very special girl. She loves to play cricket. She just did something truly wonderful! She made history! It was like a dream for her. She made so many runs. She scored so many points. It was a really, really big score. Everyone is so proud of Fanny. She is making Rwanda famous for cricket. This is a very exciting story for all of us. Let's learn more about Fanny! She is an inspiration. She shows us that big dreams can come true.

Meet Fanny: Our Cricket Hero!

Fanny is a young girl. She is only 15 years old. Can you believe that? Fifteen! That's not much older than some of your big brothers or sisters. Even though she is young, she is very good at cricket. She works very hard. She practices a lot. She loves playing with her friends. She loves being part of a team. Fanny plays for her country, Rwanda. She wears her country's colors. She plays with a bat and a ball. She is a batter. A batter is someone who hits the ball. She runs to score points. Fanny is very fast. She is very strong. She is a true cricket hero! Her friends and family cheer for her. Her country cheers for her too. **Did you know?** Rwanda is a country in East Africa. It is known as the "Land of a Thousand Hills" because it has many beautiful hills!

What is Cricket?

Hmm, what is cricket? It's a fun game! Imagine a game a bit like baseball, but also different. You have a bat and a ball. Two teams play against each other. One team tries to hit the ball and score runs. The other team tries to get them out. They try to catch the ball. They try to hit special sticks called "wickets." When you hit the ball far, you can run. Each run is a point! The team with the most points wins. Cricket is played all over the world. It is very popular in many countries. It is getting more and more popular in Africa. Fanny loves cricket very much. She loves hitting the ball with her bat. **Did you know?** Cricket bats are usually made of wood! They are very strong to hit the ball far.

What is a Century?

Now, let's talk about what Fanny did. She made a "century." Wow, what does that mean? In cricket, a century means you scored 100 runs! Yes, one hundred points all by yourself! It's very hard to do. You have to hit the ball many, many times. You have to run a lot. You have to be careful not to get out. When a player makes a century, it is a very big deal. Everyone claps for them. It shows they are a super good player. Fanny scored 100 runs. Or even more! She was so good. She kept hitting the ball. She kept scoring points. It was amazing to watch! She made 100 runs and did not get out. That is super special.

What is T20 Cricket?

Fanny played a special kind of cricket game. It is called "T20." T20 means that each team gets to bat for 20 "overs." What is an "over"? An over is when a bowler throws the ball six times. So, in T20, the game is faster. It's more exciting! Players try to hit the ball very hard. They try to score points very quickly. T20 games don't last all day. They are quick and fun! Fanny played in a T20 international game. "International" means she played against a team from another country. She was playing for Rwanda against another country. She played her best in this fast game. And she scored a century! In a fast T20 game! That's extra special! **Did you know?** The fastest T20 game can finish in about three hours! Like watching a long movie!

A Dream Come True!

When Fanny scored her century, she said it felt "like a dream." Can you imagine? When you dream of doing something big, and then it happens! That's how Fanny felt. She worked hard for this. She played with all her heart. And then, she reached 100 runs. The crowd cheered so loud! Her teammates were so happy for her. Her coach smiled. Fanny made everyone proud. She must have felt so happy inside. It was a wonderful, wonderful moment for her. A dream really came true on the cricket field that day. She was so young, only 15! To do something so big at such a young age is truly amazing.

Playing for Rwanda!

Fanny is not just playing for herself. She is playing for her country, Rwanda. When she wears her uniform, she represents all the people of Rwanda. She shows the world how talented Rwandan athletes are. She shows how strong and determined African girls can be. Her teammates are also proud. They all work together. They help each other. When Fanny plays well, it makes her whole team stronger. It makes her whole country happy. People in Rwanda are talking about Fanny. They are celebrating her big achievement. She is a hero for her country. She is putting Rwanda on the world map for cricket. **Did you know?** Rwanda's national animal is the leopard! Leopards are known for being fast and strong, just like Fanny!

Inspiring Girls Everywhere!

Fanny's story is so important because it inspires other children. Especially girls! She shows that girls can be strong. Girls can be champions. Girls can play sports and achieve great things. Maybe some girls in Rwanda or other parts of Africa will see Fanny and say, "I want to play cricket too!" Or, "I want to follow my dreams, just like Fanny!" She is a role model. A role model is someone you look up to. Someone who shows you what is possible. Fanny shows us that hard work pays off. She shows us that with passion, you can achieve anything. She encourages all young girls to try their best. **Did you know?** Sports like cricket help you stay healthy and strong! They also teach you about teamwork and fair play.

Keep Dreaming Big!

Fanny Utagushimaninde's story reminds us all to keep dreaming big. No matter how young you are. No matter where you come from. If you work hard, if you believe in yourself, you can do amazing things. Fanny's century was a big step for her. It was a big step for women's cricket in Rwanda. And it was a big step for girls' sports in Africa. Let's cheer for Fanny! Let's cheer for all the young dreamers out there. Keep playing, keep learning, and keep shining bright! Fanny is a true star, and we are so happy to celebrate her! What a wonderful achievement for our young African hero!
summary: Fanny Utagushimaninde, a 15-year-old girl from Rwanda, made history by scoring 100 runs in a cricket game, becoming the youngest woman to achieve this big goal.
whyMatters: This is important because Fanny shows all children, especially girls in Africa, that they can work hard and achieve amazing dreams in sports, making their country proud.

originalTitle: Iran war is creating ‘heightened risks of instability across countries in A
title: Hello Africa! Why Big Problems Far Away Can Touch Us Too!
content:
Hello, Super Reporters of Africa!

Hi everyone! It's your friendly kid-friendly news reporter here, ready to share some very important news with you. We're going to talk about something that might sound a little bit grown-up, but I promise to explain it in super simple words. It's about how things happening very far away can sometimes touch our lives right here in Africa. Are you ready to be amazing news detectives with me? Let's go!

What's This Big News About?

Okay, so there's a country called Iran. It's a country far, far away from Africa, across big oceans and lands. Sometimes, countries have very big fights, like really big arguments that turn into fighting. We call these fights 'wars'. A war is when people from different countries are fighting each other. It's a very sad thing, and it makes people worried. Someone very clever, a smart person named David Owiro, who knows a lot about how countries work together, has been talking about this. He says that this war far away, in Iran, can create some problems for countries here in Africa. He says African countries are 'vulnerable' to these problems. 'Vulnerable' means we might feel the effects of these problems more easily. Imagine a small boat on a big, wavy ocean – it's more vulnerable to the waves than a big, strong ship. That's a bit like what he means. Did you know? Africa is a giant continent, the second-largest in the whole world! It has 54 different countries, each with its own special people and stories!

How Can a Fight Far Away Affect Us?

That's a very good question! How can a problem happening so far away make a difference here? Think of it like this: if you throw a small stone into a big pond, it makes little ripples, right? Those ripples travel all the way to the edge of the pond. A war far away can create 'economic consequences'. 'Economic consequences' just means problems or effects that have to do with money and how much things cost in the shops. When there's a big fight like a war, it can make things less calm in the world. And when the world is less calm, it can make some important things that we all need, like oil and food, harder to get or more expensive. This is why David Owiro is helping us understand what might happen here in Africa. Did you know? Experts like David Owiro study how different countries are connected through trade and money. They help leaders make smart choices for their people.

Let's Talk About Oil!

One of the biggest things that can be affected by a war far away is oil. What is oil? Oil is like a special, dark liquid that comes from deep under the ground. It's super important because it's used for many things. It's the fuel that makes cars, buses, and big trucks go. It also helps make electricity for our homes and schools. Oil helps farms run their machines and factories make our clothes and toys. When there's a war, sometimes it's harder to get oil from where it is found to where it needs to go. Or, countries might start using more of it for their own needs during the war. When something becomes harder to get, or there is less of it, it usually costs more money to buy. Imagine if there were only a few bananas left in the shop – they might cost more because everyone wants them! Did you know? Many African countries have their own oil reserves, but the price of oil is decided by how much is available around the whole world!

Why Do Expensive Oil and Food Go Together?

Now, how does expensive oil connect to the food we eat? Think about your yummy lunch or dinner. How did the food get to your plate? Well, first, farmers grew it. Then, big trucks carried it from the farms to the markets, and then to the shops near your home. These trucks need fuel to move, and that fuel is often made from oil. So, if oil costs more money, then the trucks cost more money to run. This means the people who sell food might have to make the food cost a bit more too, just to cover their expenses. It's like a chain reaction! If the transport costs more, then the food at the shop might cost more. This can make it harder for families to buy all the food they need. This is a big part of those 'economic consequences' we talked about earlier. Did you know? Africa grows an amazing variety of foods, including maize (corn), millet, cassava, and delicious fruits like mangoes and pineapples!

What is 'Instability'?

Another important word David Owiro talks about is 'instability'. What does that mean? 'Instability' means things are not calm, steady, or safe. Imagine a chair that is wobbly – it's unstable, and you wouldn't feel safe sitting on it. When prices go up, especially for important things like food and fuel, people can become worried. This can make things feel less steady and calm in a country. When prices are too high, it can be hard for governments to do everything they want, like building new schools, making hospitals better, or making sure everyone has enough water. They might have less money to spend on these important things. Keeping things 'stable' is super important because it helps everyone feel safe and lets countries grow and become stronger. Did you know? When a country is stable, it's easier for children to go to school, for businesses to grow, and for families to live happily!

Why Africa is Special

Africa is a vibrant and amazing continent, full of brilliant people and beautiful places. But many of our countries are still busy building and growing. This means that sometimes, problems like a war far away can affect us more. It's like if you're building a tall tower with your blocks; if someone bumps the table, your tower might wobble more than a super strong, finished building. African countries might not have a lot of extra money saved up to help everyone if prices suddenly go very high. But here's the good news: Africa is also very strong! We have clever leaders and communities that work together. Understanding these problems is the first step to finding good solutions. It means that everyone needs to be extra smart about how we use our money and resources. Did you know? Africa is home to incredible wildlife, beautiful natural landscapes, and many different languages and cultures!

What Can We Do?

Even though these are big, grown-up problems, learning about them is a really important step. When we understand what's happening, we can be more prepared. Adults and leaders in African countries are already working very hard. They talk to leaders in other countries, and they try to find the best ways to protect our people and keep our countries safe and strong. We, as super reporters and super kids, can also help by learning, asking questions, and understanding our world. We can think about not wasting food, saving water, and helping our communities. Every little bit of care and understanding makes a difference. Did you know? When countries work together to solve big problems, it's called 'international cooperation', and it makes the world a better place!

Let's Wrap Up!

So, remember, a war far away in a country like Iran can send ripples all the way to Africa. These ripples can make things like oil and food cost a bit more money, and this can make things feel a little less steady for a while. But Africa is strong, and we have many clever people who are working hard to keep our homes and countries safe, stable, and happy. Thank you for being such amazing listeners and super reporters today! Keep learning, keep asking questions, and keep shining bright, Africa! Bye for now!
